items.correct: More distant galaxies recede faster - their recession velocity is proportional to distance.
items.misconception: All galaxies move at the same speed.
items.random_wrong: Galaxies only move in random directions.
items.why: Students miss the proportionality. The expansion isn't motion through space - space itself stretches, so widely separated galaxies appear to move apart faster than nearer ones.

items.q: What is the current best estimate for the age of the universe?
items.why: Students may hold non-scientific views or guess. The current consensus from CMB measurements and stellar ages is approximately 13.8 billion years - the time since the Big Bang.

items.q: What defines a star as being on the 'main sequence'?
items.correct: It fuses hydrogen into helium in its core.
items.misconception: It is the largest stage of a star's life.
items.why: Students think main sequence = most spectacular. It's defined by the FUEL: hydrogen fusion. Stars spend most of their lives there because hydrogen is plentiful and fusion is stable.

items.q: Radius of the observable universe in Gly?
items.why: It's 46. “1.4” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Filaments and voids. “WWW” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Radius of the observable universe in billions of light-years (rounded)?
items.why: Exact answer: 46 Gly. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
